title: Needle-felted bat
subtitle: A pipistrelle bat in dark brown merino with felt wings and amber resin eyes
excerpt: Build a small common pipistrelle bat in dark brown and black merino, with stretched fabric wings and a wrinkled, slightly grumpy face. The finished bat is about 8 cm across the wingspan and hangs well from a cord.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional needle-felting practice. Small bat forms are a popular autumn British craft project; the wing construction using felt fabric follows standard guild workshop technique.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method using barbed needles to tangle wool fibres together without heat or water. Each stab locks fibres into a progressively firmer mass.
Wool fibre drawn into a continuous ribbon. The standard input material for needle felting.
Lightly felted wool sheet that holds together but remains pliable. Used here to cut bat wings from a flat sheet rather than needling them individually.

content.text: has a round, slightly flattened body and wide wings cut from
sheet rather than needle felted individually. Dark brown

gives the body its colour; the wings are cut from dark or black prefelt and attached with a few needle-felting stitches at the shoulder. This is a good first project for anyone who has done one or two simpler needle-felted animals already.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Keep a finger guard on the supporting hand at all times.

items.name: Black or very dark brown prefelt sheet
Small amber resin eyes or black seed beads

content.text: Roll a small amount of dark brown roving into a tight ball about 4 cm across. Needle felt firmly, rotating the ball every few minutes, until it is round and dense. The finished body is slightly flatter on the belly side than the back: press it gently face-down on the mat for the last few minutes of needling to establish this.

Draw a bat-wing outline on the prefelt sheet: two triangular wings connected at the top, each roughly 10 cm long and 6 cm wide at the base, with a slightly scalloped lower edge. Cut out with scissors. The prefelt holds the cut edge without fraying. If no prefelt is available, wet-felt a thin flat panel from black roving before this step and let it dry completely.

Lay the wing piece across the body so the connecting strip runs along the spine. Needle felt along the spine only, securing the wings at the top while leaving the edges free so they can spread. Cut two small triangular ears from prefelt and needle felt them firmly onto the top of the head.

Needle two small cream or pale beige patches above the muzzle for the eye surrounds, then stitch amber resin eyes or black seed beads in the centres of these patches. Pull the thread tight so the eyes sit close to the surface. Needle a small blunt snout shape in the centre of the face using a wisp of dark roving.

items.problem: The wings stand straight out rather than drooping naturally
items.cause: The prefelt is too thick and stiff
items.fix: Use a thinner prefelt sheet or wet-felt a lighter panel. A thinner wing will relax into a more natural downward curve once it is attached.

items.problem: The wing keeps detaching from the body
items.cause: Only the surface fibres of the wing were joined to the body
items.fix: Needle through the full thickness of the wing into the body, not just skimming the surface. Several firm stabs along the spine will hold it securely.
